topic: work frustrations — no telehealth, long patient drives
facts_disclosed: Works at Chinle IHS, no telehealth

turns.content: so i need to vent about something and maybe you can help me figure out what to do about it. i'm tired of watching my patients suffer because the system doesn't care
I'm here to listen. That sounds really frustrating. Can you tell me more about what's going on — what system, what you're seeing?

i'm a nurse practitioner out at the IHS clinic in chinle. on the navajo nation. you know what IHS is right. indian health service. supposed to serve our people but honestly it feels like we're an afterthought half the time

I'm familiar with IHS, and I've heard this kind of frustration from other healthcare workers in the system. Chronic underfunding has been a problem for decades. What's the specific issue you're dealing with right now?

we don't have telehealth. nothing. no virtual visits, no remote monitoring, nothing. every single patient has to drive in to be seen. and out here that's not like driving across town. some of my patients are driving an hour and a half on dirt roads just to sit with me for fifteen minutes

An hour and a half on dirt roads for a 15-minute appointment — that's a serious access barrier. And for patients managing chronic conditions who need regular check-ins, that's not sustainable. Has there been any movement at all from IHS on getting telehealth set up, or is it completely off the table?

they keep talking about it. you know how it is. meetings about meetings. someone from the regional office comes out, takes notes, leaves. nothing changes. meanwhile my elders are bouncing around on washboard roads in january because they need a blood pressure check. it's not right

It's not right. And it puts an unfair burden on the people who are already the most vulnerable — elders, people without reliable vehicles, anyone with mobility issues. The irony is that telehealth is relatively cheap compared to the cost of missed appointments and emergency visits that happen when people can't make the drive.

Have you thought about putting together a case to present to the regional office? Sometimes having the numbers — missed appointment rates, average patient drive times, comparison costs — can move the needle where general complaints don't.

i've thought about it but honestly when do i have time. i see patients all day, the clinic is always short staffed, and by the time i get home i'm done. the rez has two seasons, mud and dust, and right now it's mud season so everything takes twice as long

That's the trap — you're too busy dealing with the consequences of the problem to have time to fix the problem. A few thoughts that might make it more manageable:

1. Start small — keep a simple log for one month. Every patient who mentions their drive time or who missed an appointment because of distance, just write it down. Date, distance, outcome. That alone is powerful data.

2. See if there's a public health student or intern who could pull the missed appointment data from your clinic records and put together a one-page brief. That takes it off your plate.

3. Look for allies — other providers at the clinic who feel the same way. A group request carries more weight than one person.

Would any of those feel doable given your schedule?

the log idea is practical. i could do that. just a notebook by my desk. and you're right there are a couple other providers who are just as frustrated. dr. yazzie has been complaining about this longer than i have

That's a good start. You and Dr. Yazzie together already makes it a pattern, not just one person venting. Keep the notebook simple — patient initials, how far they drove, what the visit was for, whether it could have been done virtually. After a month you'll have real evidence.

And when you're ready to bring it up the chain, frame it as patient safety and access, not staff complaints. The regional office responds better to data about outcomes than frustration about conditions. You shouldn't have to do this work on top of everything else, but you clearly care enough to try.
ahéhee'. that means thank you. i do care. been doing this a long time and i'm not going to stop fighting for my people. i'll start that notebook tomorrow

Ahéhee' for trusting me with this. Your patients are lucky to have someone who won't stop pushing for better. Start the notebook tomorrow and give it a month — then reach out to Dr. Yazzie and see if they'll co-sign a brief. Small steps, but real ones. Good luck out there.
